title: Glow Census
desc: The living lights along Mount Pillar's inner ramps are drifting dim, and the evening traffic depends on them.

steps.text:
Mount Pillar changes character when the main work cycle starts winding down. The tunnel walls do not brighten all at once. Instead, the glow beds built into ledges along the inner ramps wake in slow bands of blue and green, one level answering the next until the whole hive seems to breathe light through stone. Tonight one section of the central spiral is lagging behind, and creatures are already looking up from the traffic lanes with the practical irritation reserved for infrastructure that was supposed to behave.

A glow-tender hands you a wax slate marked with ramp sectors and a pouch of mineral dust for the hungrier beds. "The upper galleries are fading near the noble approach," she says. "The lower terraces have runoff choking the feed channels. Take the high walk and mark the weak panels, or take the lower edge and clear the channels before the evening shift fills the ramps."

options.label: Walk the upper galleries and tend the dim beds
Clear the lower feed channels before traffic builds

steps.text:
The upper gallery curves around the main shaft high above the work lanes, narrow enough to keep you careful and open enough to show most of Mount Pillar at once. Below you, lines of blue light thread around ramps, bridges, and chamber mouths. Farther down, labor courts glow soft as tide pools. Farther up, the stone narrows toward the noble levels where the light is steadier, cooler, more deliberate. Up here the hive does not look dug. It looks grown.

The dim beds themselves are simple enough to fix. A few have gone hungry where the last dusting was too light. Others are crusted over by mineral film and need to be brushed open so moisture can reach the living surface again. You work from sector to sector, dusting, scraping, and marking the slate while the light strengthens under your hands. By the time the first dense wave of evening traffic reaches the spiral, the whole inner ramp is lit in calm bands again. The glow-tender checks your slate, then the shaft beyond it, and allows herself one small nod. "Good," she says. "Now the hive can see itself properly."

options.label: Hand over the slate and watch the ramps brighten

steps.text:
The lower terraces are wetter, warmer, and busier even before the evening traffic starts. Thin trickles from the humid chambers above feed the glow beds through shallow cut channels, but the channels have picked up enough mineral scale to turn a smooth flow into scattered drips. You kneel with a scraper and work section by section, clearing the pale crust until the water begins moving like it was meant to.

As each channel opens, the nearest glow bed answers almost immediately. What had been patchy blue becomes full color, the living surface brightening in a slow roll that passes from ledge to ledge and then upward into the higher ramps beyond your sight. It is maintenance work at ground level, wet knees and numb fingers and mineral grit under your nails, yet the result spreads through the whole shaft like a signal. When the glow-tender comes to inspect, the lower terraces are carrying light cleanly again. She looks up toward the brightening spiral overhead and says, "There. One clear channel, then the next. That is how Mount Pillar keeps itself alive."

options.label: Rinse the scraper and leave the channels running
